content.problemStatement: Given an integer **N**, Chef wants to find the smallest positive integer **M** such that the bitwise XOR of **M** and **M+1** is **N**. If no such **M** exists output **-1**.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains a single integer N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the smallest positive integer M such that M XOR (M+1) equals N. If no such M exists, print -1.

sampleTestCases.explanation: 4 and 5 are not of the form 2^t - 1, therefore no positive M exists.
1 would give M=0 which is not positive; 2 and 6 are not all-ones numbers.

solution.approach: The key insight here is to recognize that M XOR (M+1) will result in a number that has all bits set to the left of the rightmost set bit in M (including the rightmost set bit itself) and all bits set to 0 to the right of the rightmost set bit in M. This pattern resembles the binary representation of numbers one less than a power of two (2^k - 1). Thus, for M XOR (M+1) to equal N, N must be of the form 2^k - 1. We can check this condition by verifying if N is one less than a power of two and if its binary representation consists of all ones (which can be done by checking if (N & (N+1)) equals 0). If N satisfies these conditions, then M can be calculated as (N-1)/2.
solution.timeComplexity: O(1) per test case, O(T) total. No precomputation is needed.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — The space used does not grow with the size of the input, making it constant.
